hero.tagline: Montana's small claims court offers a streamlined process for disputes up to $7,000, with hearings set within 40 days of filing.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

annual_filing_cap.notes: A party may not file more than 10 claims in a calendar year, except claims involving shoplifting.

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $7,000 cap, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the limit. Instead, you must file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Montana allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $7,000. The statute of limitations is 8 years for written contracts and 5 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $5,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never completed the work.
claims.notes: You have 8 years from the date of breach to file. Bring the signed contract, payment records, and any correspondence.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend $3,000 to a friend, who promised to repay in 6 months but hasn't.
claims.notes: You have 5 years from the date of breach to file. Provide evidence like emails, texts, or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $2,500 to a colleague who agreed to repay in 3 months but hasn't.
claims.notes: Depending on whether the agreement was written (8 years) or oral (5 years), file within the appropriate statute of limitations. Gather any written agreement, payment records, and communications.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $6,000 but missed payments.
claims.notes: File within 8 years of the default. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $4,500 worth of consulting services, invoiced the client, but haven't been paid.
claims.notes: For written agreements, file within 8 years; for oral, within 5 years. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any related correspondence.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $3,200 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: File within 5 years of the last transaction. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $6,500 in unpaid rent over the past 4 months.
claims.notes: File within 5 years of the missed payment. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any notices sent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid a $1,200 bill with a check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: After providing a 30-day notice, you can file for the check amount plus damages. Include the bounced check, notice sent, and any responses.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $30 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $5,000 loan, the primary borrower defaulted, and you had to pay.
claims.notes: File within 8 years if the agreement was written. Provide the loan agreement, proof of your payments, and any communications.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Montana's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $7,000, including issues with products, services, and false advertising.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $2,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of discovering the defect. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communications with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $1,500 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and photos of the work.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $3,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and refuses a refund.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the repair. Bring the repair invoice, any warranties, and records of subsequent issues.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $6,000 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods
claims.example: You paid $1,200 for furniture that was never delivered, and the vendor won't refund.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the expected delivery date. Bring the purchase agreement, payment proof, and any correspondence.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A gym continued to charge your account $500 after you canceled your membership.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation proof, bank statements, and any communications.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $6,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of purchase. Bring the sales contract, any warranties, and repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $1,000 with features it didn't have, leading you to purchase it under false pretenses.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the purchase. Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and any communications.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Montana's small claims court addresses property damage claims up to $7,000, including vehicle collisions and damage to personal property.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,500 in damage to your car and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the accident. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,000 in damage, and they won't cover the cost.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the incident. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communications with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $5,500 worth of damage to your rental unit beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of discovering the damage. Bring the lease agreement, photos, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog chewed up your $1,800 outdoor furniture, and they refuse to compensate you.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the incident. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communications with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $3,000 painting with a friend for safekeeping, and it was returned damaged.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of discovering the damage. Bring photos, repair estimates, and any agreements made.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Montana's small claims court handles monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $7,000, including issues with security deposits and unpaid rent.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification after you moved out.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the move-out date. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communications.

claims.name: Landlord didn't provide itemized deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the move-out date. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communications.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your rental had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it, causing you to incur $2,500 in alternative housing costs.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the incident. Bring the lease agreement, photos, repair requests, and receipts for alternative housing.

claims.name: Landlord kept personal property after move-out
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your $1,500 bicycle and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the move-out date. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communications.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your $3,000 worth of belongings.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the incident. Bring the lease agreement, photos, and any communications.

claims.name: Landlord didn't return prepaid rent after early termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent totaling $2,400, terminated the lease early, and the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the termination. Provide the lease agreement, payment records, and any communications.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $6,500 in unpaid rent over the past 4 months.
claims.notes: File within 5 years of the missed payment. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any notices sent.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater for $1,200 after the landlord failed to do so and deducted it from your rent, but the landlord is now suing for unpaid rent.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the repair. Provide the lease agreement, repair receipts, and any communications.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Montana's small claims court addresses wage disputes up to $7,000, including unpaid wages and final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $3,500 for hours worked but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the due date. Bring pay stubs, timesheets, and any communications.

claims.name: Employer didn't provide final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer withheld your final paycheck of $2,000.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of termination. Provide termination notice, pay stubs, and any communications.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $4,500 in sales commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the due date. Bring commission agreements, sales records, and any communications.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime worth $1,200, but your employer didn't compensate you.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the due date. Provide timesheets, pay stubs, and any communications.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Montana's small claims court handles personal claims up to $7,000, including minor injuries and property disputes.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a neighbor's icy sidewalk, resulting in $2,500 in medical bills.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the injury. Bring medical records, bills, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend your $1,800 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the demand for return. Provide proof of ownership, any agreements, and communications.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $3,000 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the theft. Bring proof of ownership, police reports, and any communications.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Montana's small claims court addresses vehicle-related disputes up to $7,000, including mechanic disputes and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and refuses a refund.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the repair. Bring the repair invoice, any warranties, and records of subsequent issues.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from your apartment complex without proper notice, resulting in $1,200 in fees.
claims.notes: File within 2 years of the towing. Provide photos, towing receipts, and any communications.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court does not handle eviction cases; these are addressed in Justice Court under regular civil procedures.
Disputes involving the ownership of real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims involving defamation, such as libel or slander, are not handled in small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are not within the purview of small claims court.
Disputes involving wills and estates are handled in probate court, not small claims court.
Small claims court does not have jurisdiction over claims against federal entities.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the scope of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring a party to do or refrain from doing something.
Disputes over workers' compensation are handled by the Workers' Compensation Court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; the focus is on compensatory damages.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $30 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are generally not recoverable in small claims court.

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing varies by county; check with the local Justice Court.

frequent_filer.notes: A party may not file more than 10 claims in a calendar year, except claims involving shoplifting.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the local court.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad checks, a 30-day written notice is required before filing a claim.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from the security deposit within 30 days of lease termination.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims.

forms.name: Small Claims Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Order of Court and Notice to Defendant
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the claim and the scheduled hearing date.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Allows the defendant to file a claim against the plaintiff in response to the original complaint.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Affidavit of Inability to Pay
forms.description: Used to request a waiver of filing fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff or constable
methods.description: A sheriff or constable personally delivers the complaint and notice to the defendant.

methods.name: Service by process server
methods.description: A private process server delivers the complaint and notice to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Montana's long-arm statute and may require additional steps.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court guidance for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant is not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to support the claim.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: The motion must be filed within 10 days of the default judgment.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds $7,000, the case may be transferred to a court with appropriate jurisdiction.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are not allowed unless both parties are represented.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial, typically lasting 30-60 minutes.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter through the court clerk well in advance of the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks.

appeals.type_notes: Appeals are limited to questions of law; no new evidence is considered.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 10 days.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of execution with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when the debtor is employed with a regular income.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment amount.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may delay satisfaction until property is sold.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize the sheriff to seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property.
methods.description: Obtain a writ from the court, and the sheriff seizes and sells property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has valuable non-exempt assets.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; renewal extends the judgment for another 10 years.

debtor_exam_form.name: Order for Examination of Judgment Debtor

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among judgment creditors is generally determined by the order of filing or recording the judgment.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney; an officer or authorized employee may represent the business.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must register the name with the Secretary of State.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may authorize an employee to represent the business in small claims court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face limitations in enforcing contracts in court.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect information can lead to dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the defendant's legal name and address before filing; for businesses, check the Secretary of State's records.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the claim limit
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing a claim over $7,000 in small claims court will result in dismissal or transfer to a higher court.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim does not exceed the $7,000 limit; if it does, consider waiving the excess or filing in a higher court.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failing to serve the defendant correctly can delay your case or lead to dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Montana's rules for service of process, using authorized individuals and methods.

pitfalls.title: Not preparing sufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can result in losing your case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect the judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a case doesn't guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods like wage garnishment and property liens, and be prepared to implement them.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, attorneys are not allowed in Montana small claims court unless both parties are represented. The process is designed for individuals to represent themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Hearings are typically scheduled within 40 days of filing the claim, and decisions are often made on the spot or shortly thereafter.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Montana small claims court is $7,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal the judgment to the District Court within 10 days. Appeals are limited to questions of law.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Montana is valid for 10 years and can be renewed for an additional 10 years.

state_specific_notes: Montana's small claims court does not allow attorneys unless both parties are represented.
The maximum claim amount in Montana small claims court is $7,000.
Hearings are typically scheduled within 40 days of filing the claim.
A party may not file more than 10 claims in a calendar year, except claims involving shoplifting.
Appeals must be filed within 10 days and are limited to questions of law.

interesting_findings: Montana's small claims court emphasizes self-representation and informal procedures to make the legal process more accessible.
The state has specific statutes governing various aspects of small claims, ensuring clarity and consistency in proceedings.

sources.citation: Montana Courts: Courts of Limited Jurisdiction

sources.citation: Montana Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Act
